# Geometry of Data


Thinking geometrically is one of the most productive habits in data science.
Each observation can be viewed as a point in a feature space. Similarity,
distance, clustering, projection, and dimension reduction are all geometric
ideas.

## Distance

For vectors $x,y \in \mathbb{R}^p$, the Euclidean distance is

$$
d(x,y) = \|x-y\|_2.
$$

When features are measured on very different scales, Euclidean distance may be
misleading. Standardization can help.

## Angles and cosine similarity

The cosine of the angle between vectors is

$$
\cos(\theta) = \frac{x^T y}{\|x\|_2\|y\|_2}.
$$

In text mining and recommendation systems, cosine similarity often provides a
better measure of resemblance than raw Euclidean distance.

## Projections

If $u$ is a nonzero vector, then the projection of $x$ onto $u$ is

$$
\operatorname{proj}_u(x) = \frac{x^T u}{u^T u}u.
$$

Projection is a central idea in least squares and principal component analysis.

::: {.theorem}
The vector $x - \operatorname{proj}_u(x)$ is orthogonal to $u$.
:::

## Visual example

```{r, fig.cap="A cloud of points in two-dimensional feature space"}
set.seed(1)
x1 <- rnorm(100)
x2 <- 0.7 * x1 + rnorm(100, sd = 0.5)
plot(x1, x2, xlab = "Feature 1", ylab = "Feature 2", pch = 19, cex = 0.7)
```

## Exercises

1. Compute the distance between $(1,2)$ and $(4,6)$.
2. Compute cosine similarity for $(1,1)$ and $(2,2)$.
3. Project $(3,4)$ onto $(1,0)$.
4. Explain why scaling can affect nearest-neighbor methods.
